data.title: BN(0) visa holders deserve clarity on the pathway to a new life in the UK
data.description:
Britain’s crackdown on immigration accelerated last week with the unveiling of radical reforms which will make it more difficult for those embarking on a new life in the country to settle there. The sweeping changes will apply not only to asylum seekers but to migrants who have entered the UK lawfully since 2021. The goalposts are moving.
Tighter restrictions for people who relocate to the UK through legal routes, such as for work or study, are the subject of a 12-week consultation.
The aim, according to the government, is to prevent the dangerous deepening of divisions in society arising from unprecedented levels of immigration in recent years.
New requirements for those arriving on various visas are intended to ensure migrants given indefinite leave to remain have “earned it”.
Applicants will have to show they have made a meaningful economic contribution while integrating and engaging with British society. Good character and high English language standards will also be needed.
Most will have to wait 10 years for settlement, a status allowing successful applicants to live, work, study and access benefits. This new baseline is double the length of the current period.
But there will be a sliding scale. High earners, entrepreneurs and “the brightest and best” talent can, under the proposals, have their qualifying period cut to three years. Those who claim benefits, however, face a wait of up to 20 years. It will be like running in a race and not knowing the distance to the finishing line.
The extended 10-year baseline, first announced in May, was a source of concern for the more than 160,000 Hong Kong people who have moved to Britain under the British National (Overseas) visa scheme, introduced in 2021 in response to the city’s national security law.
British officials had refused to say whether the new qualifying period would apply to them. Last week, it was revealed the Hongkongers would be exempted. Better late than never. The existing five-year wait will remain.
This is just as well, as 92,000 BN(O) visa holders are expected to apply for indefinite leave to remain next year. They are among 195,000 due to seek settlement by 2030. Once settled status is secured, citizenship can follow a year later.
It would have been outrageous to extend the period Hong Kong people, who have made the difficult decision to uproot and start a new life in the UK, must wait for the greater certainty and benefits settlement brings. A campaign against such a move, which saw protests, a parliamentary debate and a petition signed by more than 108,000 people, has proved successful.
Arrivals from Hong Kong were described by Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood as having a “uniquely strong attachment to this country”, equating them with relatives of British citizens. This reflects the city’s history as a British colony, until its return to China in 1997.
The consultation paper reaffirmed the government’s “steadfast support for members of the Hong Kong community in the UK.” It stated: “We fully recognise the significant contribution that Hongkongers have already made in the UK and the role they will continue to play in the years ahead.”
This is good to hear. But any celebrations among BN(O) visa holders were short lived. The consultation paper sets minimum requirements for settled status. The Home Office has confirmed that these are intended to apply to the BN(O) scheme.
This would mean Hong Kong people, along with all other migrants, might – subject to the consultation – be required to earn £12,570 (HK$128,000) annually for three to five years before their application. They would also qualify if they have “an alternative amount”. But it is not clear what this means. There is a need for clarity.
The government’s objective is to ensure migrants contribute to its coffers by paying income tax. But many BN(O) visa holders live on the savings or investments they brought with them from Hong Kong. Some are looking after children and part-time work is not easy to find. It makes no sense to exclude people in this position from securing indefinite leave to remain. They are most unlikely to become a burden on the state.
Another concern is the raising of the standard of English language competency required. This is to be stepped up a grade, from B1 to B2, under the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages. A Home Office spokesperson said the government believed the “vast majority” of BN(O) visa holders are already at that level. The Hongkongers are generally better educated than the average British resident. Many of them will have no problem with this requirement.
But others are understandably anxious that despite efforts to improve their English, the new level will be beyond them. The battle to acquire a better command of the language has been one of the concerns raised by members of the BN(O) community, as they seek to integrate and find work.
The government is placing the responsibility for meeting the requirements on the migrants. But it also has a role to play. More needs to be done to ensure they can access English language classes and to provide support for those seeking jobs which are commensurate with their qualifications and experience.
Hong Kong people are precisely the sort of migrants the government says it is looking for. It has noted the contribution they are already making to UK society and should not place fresh obstacles in their way.
The overhaul of the system for refugees and legal migrants is being driven by political expediency. The arrival of thousands of asylum seekers on small boats from France has become one of the biggest concerns of British people, sparking protests.
Anti-immigration Reform UK is leading in the opinion polls and the Labour government’s popularity has plunged. It fears a backlash from voters in local elections next year. This is why it is adopting a hard line.
Mahmood said she was not willing to “cede this territory of secure borders to parties of the far right”. But the traditionally left-wing party must be careful not to lose its moral compass in the drive to win votes and reverse its fortunes.
Mahmood, whose parents arrived in the UK as immigrants decades ago, described fairness as being “the most fundamental of British values” and said: “We do not break our promises.” Those principles must be applied to immigrants as well as the wider public if the UK is to maintain its claimed position as “the greatest multi-ethnic, multi-faith democracy in the world”.

data.title: Why US tariff refunds are now a very real possibility … and a giant ‘mess’
data.description:
Marilyn-Joy Cerny has challenged the US government thousands of times during her 35-year career as a trade lawyer, enjoying an enviable record of recovering tariff money for importers whom Washington wrongfully charged.
But the sheer scale of potential refunds – known technically as protests – should the Supreme Court rule against US President Donald Trump’s tariff policies in the coming weeks, potentially amounting to US$100 billion or more, has given even her pause.
“I have filed so many protests in my day, I joke with my team that Customs might be saying, ‘me think she doth protest too much’,” said Cerny, managing partner with law firm Sandler, Travis & Rosenberg. “Of my protest cases that I’ve had to take to court? I would say 95, 96 per cent [were successful]. But the Supreme Court may really erode my success rate.”
During oral arguments earlier this month, several Supreme Court justices appeared sceptical of Trump’s record of collecting tariffs at will based on the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). Under the US Constitution, Congress oversees taxation, not the president.


Trump’s legal team, however, argues that the decades-old US trade deficit is a national emergency that justifies imposing tariffs on most of the world and that they are regulatory and not aimed at generating revenue.
Should the ruling go against Trump – the decision is expected within weeks, lightning speed by Supreme Court standards – Washington may have to engineer a giveback larger than the annual GDP of over 100 nations.
This would be a bureaucratic nightmare that would take a long time, involve complications and potentially more legislation, or as Supreme Court Associate Justice Amy Coney Barrett put it more demurely, be a “mess”.
It could also undercut US budget assumptions and threaten dozens of bilateral trade deals Trump has negotiated. Conservative Justice Neil Gorsuch noted, however, that this was not a justification for avoiding any particular ruling.
Trump has called any such ruling “devastating”, and claimed it would cost the country US$3 trillion, a figure difficult to verify. “It would not be possible to ever make up for that kind of a ‘drubbing’,” he added on social media. “People who are against tariffs are FOOLS!”
For traders, refund uncertainty is only the latest chapter in an exhausting drama as tariffs have soared and plummeted, deadlines have been proclaimed and yanked, with the saga played out erratically on social media.
“It’s been like a roller coaster in the dark,” said Nick Bartlett, co-founder of Wayfindr, a Hong Kong-based logistics consultancy. “Just when you think it’s over, you get hit with another spin or turn that comes at you.”
Most tariff refunds involve clerical mistakes or misclassification involving particular transactions. The US saw a broad-based refund in 1998, however, after the Supreme Court found a harbour maintenance tax unconstitutional. The result was years of litigation in a case involving just US$750 million.
Custom brokers and shipping companies like DHL and FedEx have receipts of tariff payments; the problems mount when importers try to apportion them.
Many manufacturers, importers and customers have absorbed part of the increase informally to maintain relationships and retain customers amid White House pressure to hold prices steady. Then there are the practicalities: offer a discount or a bonus, or quietly pocket the windfall and what if the money is already spent?
“And if you’re a seller on Etsy and the product is now US$5 cheaper for somebody in Duluth, are you really going to send US$5 for each sweatshirt to all 1,000 customers?” said John Ellis, president of Optics For Hire, which designs laser and related systems. “It’s a holy mess.”

Wall Street firms have reportedly offered to buy tariff claims from cash-strapped importers at discounts of up to 20 cents on the dollar, betting on a windfall.
Dirtbag Brands, which sells accessories for off-road vehicles, is owed a “meaningful” refund. But the payments have already been absorbed into pricing, freight contracts and dealer networks, complicating the task of distributing them and upending forecasting and planning across its supply chains.
“The administrative cost of tracing every import over multiple years would outweigh the refund itself for most businesses,” said Josh Salazar, Dirtbag’s chief executive.
An added twist follows when Chinese manufacturers are factored in.
Foxco, an Arizona-based e-commerce supply-chain consultancy handling imported electronic parts from China, said most of its US clients saw their tariffs apparently covered by tax credits or other offsets engineered by Beijing.
“The [Chinese government] instructed the factories to rebate the tariffs. There was no friction whatsoever,” said Andrew Fox, Foxco’s director. “If it were returned, the importer would get something of a windfall since they didn’t pay it.”
Fox believes most Chinese factory owners would let it ride, handing importers a lucky break.
“Would the Chinese factory try and claw it back? I don’t think so. Chinese people know about the world. There are no fools in China, especially those running factories,” Fox said.
“Losing customers they’ve built up over the last decade or more? That is something they’re not going to let happen.”
Facing the prospect of his signature tariff policy being deemed illegal, populist President Trump has proposed giving every American “at least” a US$2,000 handout, excluding high-income earners. Cheques could land just before the 2026 midterm elections, although this idea would likely face legislative, budgetary and legal challenges.
Henrietta Treyz, co-founder of Washington consultancy Veda Partners, for one, is sceptical.

“The odds of this happening are 10 per cent,” the former Senate staffer said. Not only would a handout cost the US Treasury some US$400 billion, more than any refunds, but the tariff revenue is now part of the US General Fund, which Congress controls.
“He does not have the authority to issue tax rebates,” Treyz said. “This is the crux of the legal argument currently pending before the Supreme Court.”
Critics say Americans would have been better served by not imposing exorbitant tariffs in the first place. Handouts also risk fuelling inflation – Covid-19 payments saw it hit a 40-year high, driving up the nation’s US$37 trillion budget deficit.
“That’s why the administration is dangling this idea,” said Michael Cramer, chief executive at Adagio Teas, which imports from China and India. “I think he’s looking to get a lot of consumers to be in favour of the tariffs and clamour for this money.”
Average effective US tariffs at 18 per cent are now at their highest level since 1934, according to the Yale Budget Lab. Striking down Trump’s tariffs could cut that to 6.5 per cent, a level not seen since Trump’s April 2 “Liberation Day” announcement.
Most traders expect the mercurial president to find other ways to charge them, however. In a possible pre-emptive move, his administration launched a Section 301 investigation last month that carries punitive tariffs tied to whether China has abided by its phase one trade commitments.
“If IEEPA gets struck down, they’ll immediately pivot to the other arrows in the quiver,” said Wayfindr’s Bartlett. “And that’s where the planning nightmare begins.”
Other potential tools include trade law sections 232, 201 and 338 justified on national security or domestic harm grounds. Most, however, require public hearings and the “process” that Trump dislikes.
Depending on the ruling, the court could split the difference by returning the case to lower courts, rendering a partial decision or limiting refunds to the case’s few plaintiffs. That possibility has seen several “me-too” lawsuits filed by importers keen to be included in any refund.


Meanwhile, the tariff hoard continues to grow.
Monthly US customs revenue jumped to US$30 billion by September from US$7 billion in January, with roughly US$151 billion collected in the second half of the financial year alone, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, while the annual revenue derived from Trump’s levies could reach US$108 billion by November 1, according to PwC.
Amid the uncertainty, importers question whether Trump’s policies are having their intended effect. Trump has claimed that tariffs have generated “record investment”, will bolster US manufacturing, factory jobs and exports and reduce the US trade deficit.
Chinese exports to the US have seen double-digit declines for seven consecutive months, with shipments in October down by over 25 per cent compared to the same period of last year, according to US data.
And while China’s overcapacity and trade surpluses may be unsustainable, traders say, long-term investments require consistent policy not seen under Trump. Others concede that the churn has forced some tough but necessary decisions. “The truth is, uncertainty is here to stay,” said John Ceng, founder of Guangdong-based metal and plastics factory Ezra.
Policies often seem ill-focused as the administration tries to reduce even low-end production or apply tariffs on products the United States cannot produce competitively, such as tea, toys and T-shirts.
Tennessee-based Vol Case and Container, which manufactures boxes for delicate scientific and musical instruments, has shifted some Chinese production back to the US. But it mostly involves higher-margin products that can absorb higher US costs, often relies on local tax breaks, and has seen the company automate to avoid adding employees even as it sources more from India and Vietnam.

“I feel, mistake after mistake, we’re just fumbling through the whole thing,” said co-owner Matteo Valles.
Even if factories return, the US has lost many basic manufacturing skills that would take years to recover.
“All of the tool and dye engineers in the US could fill a conference room,” said Fox. “All the ones in China could fill a stadium.”
Many ultimately see no immediate replacement for China, even with exorbitant tariffs. Optics for Hire recently sought alternatives for a US$10 Chinese-made prism. The quoted price was US$38 in Korea and US$290 by US military contractors, the nation’s only remaining producer.
“In most cases, nothing has changed other than higher prices for our US customers,” said Ellis. “It would be a good time to be a trade lawyer or a bonded warehouse.”

data.title: ‘Disaster’: Egyptian exhibition at Hong Kong museum descends into chaos
data.description:
A highly anticipated exhibition at the Hong Kong Palace Museum of relics from ancient Egypt descended into chaos on Saturday, with visitors complaining a confusing queuing system left them waiting for hours and many demanding a refund.
The museum apologised in the evening and promised to offer additional access for the affected visitors.
“Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums” features 250 artefacts, including a towering statue of Tutankhamun, and is the largest display yet of relics from the ancient civilisation in the city.
Egyptian Deputy Minister of Tourism and Antiquities Yomna El-Bahar came to Hong Kong for the opening on Thursday.

The nine-month exhibition is a follow-up to a highly successful one in Shanghai that came ahead of celebrations to mark 70 years of diplomatic ties between China and the Middle Eastern country next year.
Social media users shared on Saturday that visitors had to start queuing on the second floor to enter the exhibition hall on the fourth floor, only to then discover the line was reserved for taking the lifts to the third floor.
Those who reached the third floor would need to queue up again to go up one more, one user said.
Some said that when they finally reached the fourth floor, they were told they needed to get waiting tickets and return at the specified time.
“If you are planning to visit, please reserve at least two hours for queuing, because the flow management here is extremely bad,” one social media user wrote.

They slammed the museum for failing to provide clear directions and to prevent people from jumping the queue.
“We decided to cancel our tickets, but there was also a long queue at the refund counter, and it will take six to eight weeks to get a refund,” one user wrote. “I am very angry. Our family activity turned into a disaster.”
The museum announced on its social media accounts at around 6pm that all tickets were sold out and asked holders to keep waiting outside the gate and follow the staff’s instructions.
At around 8pm, the museum said it would offer the ticket holders an additional visit on any day in the coming three months.
It added that it had extended the opening hour of the exhibition by one hour until 9pm on Saturday and by two hours till 8pm on Sunday.

“We recommend visitors to plan their visit in the morning or early evening to avoid peak afternoon crowds,” it said.
“Please note that only a limited number of tickets will be available for on-site purchase, and ticket sales may be suspended during peak periods depending on visitor flow. Visitors are encouraged to plan their visit and book tickets online in advance,” it said.
“We sincerely apologise for any inconvenience caused by today’s admission arrangements. Moving forward, we will review our procedures and allocate additional staff to assist with on-site operations.”
Souvenirs, such as Egyptian cultural-themed blind boxes and black cat dolls, have become hot items on social media, and one user reported queing an hour to purchase a doll.
Souvenirs, such as Egyptian cultural-themed blind boxes and black cat dolls, have become hot items on social media, and one user reported queuing an hour to purchase a doll.
Tickets cost HK$190 (US$24) for adults and HK$95 for concessions, with other discounts and entry combinations available.
The museum expected to draw over 700,000 visitors across the nine-month period.

data.title: G20 South Africa summit wins strong China backing amid first-ever US boycott
data.description:
The G20 summit in Johannesburg opened on Saturday with a firm call from the host nation to safeguard the group’s credibility and role, despite the shadow cast by the first-ever US boycott in the forum’s history.
South Africa has, however, secured crucial diplomatic backing from China for the assembly of the world’s wealthiest and leading emerging economies.
Members should “not allow anything to diminish the value, stature and impact of the first African G20”, South African President Cyril Ramaphosa told the opening session, in what was seen as a veiled reference to Washington’s boycott and threat to veto the resolutions.
The event “carries the hopes and must reflect the aspirations of the people of this continent and of the world”, Ramaphosa said, emphasising the historic significance of the two-day summit for Africa.
The summit had “a responsibility not to allow the integrity and the credibility of the G20 to be weakened”, he added.
China has appeared to criticise the US stance, with Premier Li Qiang pledging to strengthen coordination on platforms such as the Group of 20.

Li, who is in Johannesburg for the summit, told Ramaphosa during a meeting on Friday that China stood ready to strengthen cooperation with South Africa across the G20 and Brics platforms, specifically to “uphold the multilateral trading system [and] promote reform of the global governance system”. China would also “defend the common interests of developing countries”, Li said.
China’s high-level backing ensures South Africa’s priorities, such as strengthening disaster resilience and response, debt sustainability, and processing critical minerals at the place of extraction, would remain central to the summit’s negotiations.
In his summit speech, Ramaphosa urged members to mobilise finance for a just energy transition, increasing the quality and quantity of climate finance flows to developing economies.
“The G20 needs to renew its efforts to advance debt sustainability while at the same time laying emphasis on those countries in the global south that are suffering from debt,” he said.
Li, China’s No 2 official, is representing President Xi Jinping, who has cut down his international travel schedule over the past few years.
Russian President Vladimir Putin is not attending the summit due to the International Criminal Court arrest warrant issued against him over the war in Ukraine.
The leaders of Mexico, Argentina and Indonesia have also sent representatives.

The G20 leaders adopted a declaration at the start of the summit, in a notable break from tradition where the document is usually finalised on the second day or after leaders give their remarks. But Argentina declined to adopt the document, citing concerns about some of the content.
Washington announced its total boycott earlier this month, with President Donald Trump accusing South Africa of persecuting Afrikaner farmers – labelling it “white genocide” – a claim strongly denied by the South African government.
America’s absence is part of broader unilateral moves, including the imposition of a 30 per cent reciprocal tariff on South African exports such as agricultural produce, automotive components, steel and aluminium.
The US, which is next in line for the rotating G20 presidency, also accuses South Africa of engaging in unfair trade practices and pursuing foreign policies deemed detrimental to American interests, such as its alignment with China and Russia, and strong stance on Palestine.
In contrast, Beijing has removed all tariffs for products entering the massive mainland Chinese market from every African country except eSwatini, which maintains formal diplomatic relations with Taiwan.
The diplomatic friction has been compounded by the US’ long-standing opposition to South Africa’s G20 agenda, which focuses on climate finance, debt restructuring, and trade reform, which Washington has previously dismissed as being “anti-American”.
While Trump said no American official would participate in the summit activities, Ramaphosa said on Thursday that the US had reversed its decision and talks were under way regarding sending a delegation to the G20 on the last day of the summit.
It’s South Africa’s time to shine, and change global attitudes to Africa
Steven Gruzd, South African Institute of International Affairs
However, the White House immediately dismissed the claims as false, confirming that the US would not participate, though an official from its South African embassy would attend the formal handover for the 2026 G20 presidency.
But South Africa has refused to hand over the baton to junior officials.
South Africa’s deputy government spokesman, William Baloyi, later said that the US had reached out to indicate they would be attending on the last day.
Steven Gruzd, head of the African Governance and Diplomacy Programme and Africa-Russia Project at the South African Institute of International Affairs, a Johannesburg think tank, said that it was “hugely important” for South Africa to successfully host the summit.
He noted that South Africa had championed issues of importance to the Global South and Africa, such as debt relief, taxing the super-rich, and climate matters. The G20 presidency had passed through four developing countries in a row since 2022 – Indonesia, India, Brazil and now South Africa – with issues like tackling hunger, poverty, and inequality being carried over, he added.
“It’s South Africa’s time to shine, and change global attitudes to Africa.”

Though Xi was not attending, Gruzd said China had consistently supported the G20 as a forum and publicly expressed support for South Africa’s G20 theme of “Solidarity, Equality, Sustainability”, while pledging to work with the other G20 states to build consensus on multilateralism, open trade and global development.
Sarang Shidore, director of the Global South programme at the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft, an American think tank, said he would be curious to see the language on trade, climate change, Ukraine and the Middle East emerging from the summit, given the US absence.
“I expect a stronger endorsement of WTO [World Trade Organization] norms and greater stress on climate action,” he forecast.
The meeting went into a closed-door session soon after Ramaphosa’s speech.

data.title: Platform for Hong Kong BN(O) visa holders launches campaign against new UK rules
data.description:
A UK-based platform supporting British National (Overseas) visa holders is launching a campaign to lobby against London’s proposed immigration reforms, calling them unfair and a source of “acute anxiety” for the Hong Kong community in the country.
The Sutton Hongkongers group said people who had moved to Britain from the city felt that the UK government was “moving the goalposts”, making its past pledges a “broken promise”.
The platform, founded by Richard Choi Ka-yuen, a Liberal Democrat councillor in the London borough of Sutton, urged the Labour government to exempt existing BN(O) visa holders from any new rules to obtain citizenship.
Choi released a report on Friday via his social media account, saying the group had already received 50 pieces of feedback from concerned Hongkongers within a day of the British government announcing a consultation on the reforms.
“[This reflects] the acute anxiety felt by the community,” the group said.
The UK released for public consultation earlier this week proposals imposing stricter rules for obtaining citizenship, including extending the period to 10 years that people in certain immigration routes will need to wait before being able to apply for settlement.
While the British government has maintained the five-year pathway to permanent residency for BN(O) visa holders, they will still be subject to stricter English proficiency requirements, as well as a possible annual income threshold of £12,750 for three to five years.
A petition by Sutton Hongkongers said the community strongly advocated for “grandfathering” existing visa holders.
It also said that raising the English-language standard to A-level equivalent was “an unfair moving of the goalposts”. It noted that many “older adults” had been working towards the “intermediate” level as originally required.
“Older migrants find learning a new language at an advanced academic level extremely difficult,” it said. “Moving the goalposts … creates an insurmountable barrier for many who are otherwise well-integrated and law-abiding.”
It also said the proposed income threshold would bar retirees, homemakers and students from settlement, stressing that they were originally admitted based on savings and self-sufficiency.
“If the government later introduces a requirement that BN(O) applicants must show ongoing income, such as a minimum salary level or stable employment, in order to apply for [settlement], this creates a direct contradiction with the original principles and expectations of the BN (O) scheme,” it said.
“The original BN (O) visa requires proof of financial stability, not proof of employment.”
Britain launched the BN(O) migration pathway in January 2021 in response to the Beijing-imposed national security law, approving nearly 225,000 such visas for Hongkongers and recording more than 160,000 arrivals as of September this year.
The scheme is among a number of settlement programmes covered in an overhaul of the UK’s immigration system, with the British government saying it was prioritising those contributing to the country’s economy and playing by the rules.

data.title: Ukraine, US to meet in Switzerland over Trump’s plan to end war with Russia
data.description:
Ukraine and the US will soon meet in Switzerland to discuss Washington’s plan for ending the war with Russia, which currently heeds some of Russia’s hardline demands, Kyiv said on Saturday.
US President Donald Trump gave Ukraine less than a week to approve the 28-point plan to end the nearly four-year conflict, which would see the invaded country ceding territory, cutting its army and pledging to never join Nato.
Meanwhile, Ukraine’s European allies, who were not included in drafting the agreement, were scrambling at the G20 summit in South Africa to come up with a counter-offer to Trump’s plan to strengthen Kyiv’s positions.
“In the coming days in Switzerland we are launching consultations between senior officials of Ukraine and the United States on the possible parameters of a future peace agreement,” Rustem Umerov, who is on Ukraine’s negotiating team, wrote on social media.
“This is another stage of the dialogue that has been ongoing in recent days and is primarily aimed at aligning our vision for the next steps,” added Umerov, a former defence minister, who is now the Secretary of the Security Council.
He previously led a few rounds of negotiations with Russia in Turkey, which yielded no breakthrough. This time, Zelensky appointed his top aide, Andriy Yermak, to lead the team, according to a presidential decree.
The decree said the talks will also include “representatives of the Russian Federation”.
There was no immediate confirmation from Russia whether it would join the talks.

Just peace
In a joint declaration, the G20 leaders called for a “just, comprehensive, and lasting peace”, but not only in Ukraine, but also in Sudan, the Democratic Republic of Congo and the “Occupied Palestinian Territory”.
France’s Emmanuel Macron sent a sombre message to the gathering, saying “the G20 may be coming to the end of a cycle”, adding that the grouping was struggling to resolve major crises around the world.
He referred specifically to a new unilateral US plan to end the war in Ukraine that accepts some of Russia’s hardline demands.
Shortly before that, Macron met with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer on the sidelines of the summit, boycotted by the US, to discuss a joint response to Washington’s plan.
Starmer had earlier said the aim was to “look at how we can strengthen this plan for the next phase of negotiations”.

Difficult choice
Ukraine faces one of the most challenging moments in its history, Zelensky said in an address to the nation, adding that he would propose alternatives to Trump’s proposal.
“The pressure on Ukraine is one of the hardest. Ukraine may face a very difficult choice: either the loss of dignity or the risk of losing a key partner,” Zelensky said in his address, referring to a possible break with Washington.
To end the war, the US plan envisages recognising territories controlled by Moscow as “de facto” Russian, with Kyiv pulling troops out of parts of the Donetsk region.
Ukraine would also cap its army at 600,000, rule out joining Nato and have no troops from the alliance deployed to its territory.
In return, Ukraine would get unspecified “reliable security guarantees” and a fund for reconstruction using some Russian assets frozen in foreign accounts.
While Russia would gain territory, be reintegrated into the global economy and rejoin the G8, according to a draft of the plan.
Putin said the blueprint could “lay the foundation” for a final peace settlement, but threatened more land seizures if Ukraine walked away from negotiations.
Better equipped and larger in numbers, the Russian army is slowly but steadily gaining ground across the lengthy front line.
Ukrainians were meanwhile facing one of the toughest winters since the war began, after Moscow carried out a brutal bombing campaign against energy infrastructure.
This comes as a sweeping corruption probe that unveiled corruption in the energy sector was unravelling in Kyiv, sparking public outcry.

data.title: Japan city pins hope on international cricket ground to revitalise region
data.description:
A cricket ground that meets international standards and is located north of Tokyo is attracting visitors from Japan and abroad, raising hopes that it will serve as a hub for regional revitalisation and promote a sport that has remained relatively low profile in the country.
Naoki Alex Miyaji, 47, a former Japan team player and current CEO of the Japan Cricket Association, connected the sport with Sano, Tochigi Prefecture, by establishing the organisation’s local office there in 2007. The association is now headquartered in the city.
The city opened the Sano International Cricket Ground at the former site of a prefectural high school in 2018, spending around 400 million yen (US$2.6 million), including aid from the national government.
In late October, the ground hosted the Embassy Cup, a match between the Japanese national team and a group of experienced foreign players. Nine foreign embassies in Japan supported the event, and visitors enjoyed trial cricket sessions and international cuisine.
“It’s fun because it requires a high level of defensive strategy, given batters are allowed to hit in any direction,” Naoaki Shimomura, the association’s media officer, said.

With around 300 million players worldwide, cricket is set to be included in the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles. However, there are only about 5,800 players in Japan, and its men’s and women’s national teams are ranked around 40th, according to the association.
“I want to make cricket famous and popular by winning games,” said Makoto Taniyama, a 29-year-old member of the national team, who moved from Higashiosaka, Osaka Prefecture, to Sano for a better playing environment.
Miyaji has also worked to promote the sport by organising “cricket in English” classes in schools in the city and inviting a coach from Britain.
He expressed hope of contributing to the local community, saying, “Intercultural exchange can happen in Sano as the cricket ground brings people from Japan and from around the world”.

data.title: Gunmen seize 315 in latest Nigerian mass school kidnapping
data.description:
Gunmen have kidnapped more than 300 students and teachers in one of the largest mass kidnappings in Nigeria, a Christian group said on Saturday, as security fears mounted in Africa’s most populous nation.
The early Friday raid on St Mary’s co-education school in Niger state in central Nigeria came after gunmen on Monday stormed a secondary school in neighbouring Kebbi state, abducting 25 girls.
The Christian Association of Nigeria (CAN) had earlier reported 227 people seized, but the new number came “after a verification exercise” following the early Friday mass kidnapping, and added that “The total number of victims abducted … is now 303 students and 12 teachers”.
The number of boys and girls kidnapped from St Mary’s is almost half of the school’s student population of 629.
The Nigerian government has not commented on the number of students and teachers abducted.
Niger state governor Mohammed Umar Bago told reporters on Saturday that the intelligence department and police were “doing the headcount”, and the figure would be released by the end of the day.
Bago, whose government had ordered some schools shut, also ordered the closure of all schools in his state, a day after authorities in the nearby states of Katsina and Plateau closed all theirs as a precautionary measure. The national education ministry has also ordered 47 boarding secondary schools across the country to be shut.
President Bola Tinubu has cancelled international engagements, including attending the G20 summit in Johannesburg, to handle the crisis.
The two abduction operations and an attack on a church in the west of the country, in which two people were killed, have happened since US President Donald Trump threatened military action over what he called the killing of Christians by radical Islamists in Nigeria.
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth called on Abuja to “take both urgent and enduring action to stop violence against Christians”, on Friday during talks with Nigerian National Security Adviser Nuhu Ribadu, the Pentagon said in a statement.
Nigeria is still scarred by the kidnapping of nearly 300 girls by Boko Haram jihadists at Chibok in northeastern Borno state more than a decade ago. Some of those girls are still missing.

Myriad security challenges
CAN said Reverend Bulus Dauwa Yohanna, who is also the Catholic Bishop of Kontagora Diocese under which the school falls, gave the update after visiting St Mary’s.
“After we left the school at Papiri, we decided to make calls, do a verification exercise and do further inquiries on those we had thought escaped successfully, only to discover that 88 more students were also captured after they tried to escape,” he said.
“This now makes it 303 students [male and female] including 12 teachers [4 females and 8 males] bringing the total number of abducted persons to 315,” he said in a statement.
For years, heavily armed criminal gangs have been intensifying attacks in rural areas of northwest and central Nigeria, where there is little state presence, killing thousands and conducting kidnappings for ransom.
No group has claimed the latest attacks but bandit gangs seeking ransom payments often target schools in rural areas where security is weak.

The gangs have camps in a vast forest straddling several states including Zamfara, Katsina, Kaduna, Sokoto, Kebbi and Niger.
In a separate attack on a church in western Nigeria on Tuesday, gunmen killed two people during a service that was being broadcast online. Dozens of worshippers are believed to have been abducted.
As Nigeria grapples with security challenges on several fronts, hostage-taking has spiralled nationwide and become a favoured tactic of bandit gangs and jihadists.
Although bandits have no ideological leanings and are motivated by financial gain, their increasing alliance with jihadists from the northeast has been a source of concern for authorities and security analysts.
Jihadists have for 16 years been waging an insurrection in the northeast with the aim of establishing a Caliphate.

data.title: China’s Shenzhou 20 debris crisis: a catalyst for space war – or peace?
data.description:
A tiny piece of debris triggered the most serious mishap at China’s Tiangong space station since it became operational three years ago.
Earlier this month, a crack discovered on the Shenzhou-20 spacecraft’s window forced three Chinese astronauts to remain on board Tiangong for nine extra days and eventually borrow their newly arrived colleagues’ ship to return to Earth.
The event underscores the growing threat posed by Earth’s ever-expanding cloud of orbital junk – a scare that could push China, as well as other countries including the United States, to speed up their debris tracking and removal capabilities.
Many of these systems are inherently dual-use: the same tools that can safely deorbit defunct satellites could also be repurposed to disable enemy spacecraft.
Still, some experts see a sliver of hope that this incident could serve as a much-needed wake-up call to catalyse risk-reduction measures between Beijing and Washington, and become a turning point for cooperation between the world’s two leading space powers.
While China rushes to ready the next spacecraft, Shenzhou-22, to be launched next week as the station’s new lifeboat, the country had already begun “working more seriously on space-debris issues” before the episode, said Harvard astronomer and space historian Jonathan McDowell.
In September, China launched Kaiyun-1 – the first of more than 100 planned satellites for a domestic space-situational-awareness constellation designed to reduce its reliance on US tracking data.
The constellation, run by Beijing-based Kaiyun United and broadly comparable to US Space Force space-domain-awareness assets, is expected to become a major part of China’s debris tracking capabilities once completed in 2028.


At the recently held International Astronautical Congress in Sydney, a senior Chinese space official said the country was also “researching the active removal of space debris on orbit”. The country has already demonstrated some of these capabilities, including using its Shijian-21 satellite in 2022 to tow a defunct Beidou navigation satellite into a graveyard orbit.
China is also reported to be developing other removal tools, such as robotic arms to grab dead or non-cooperative satellites, space tugs that can push or pull objects into safer orbits, and lasers that gently nudge small debris so it re-enters the atmosphere.
While many of these technologies are dual-use and could intensify great-power competition in orbit, Lincoln Hines, a Chinese space policy researcher at Georgia Tech, said the Tiangong episode could also be a “wake-up call” for collaboration, especially between China and the US.
“Risk-reduction measures could include the two countries notifying each other about potential collisions,” he wrote in an article for The Conversation on November 13. “China and the United States could also open discussions around how to safely operate satellites or remove them from orbit when they’re no longer useful.”
Such communication, though rare, has already begun. In October, Nasa space sustainability director Alvin Drew told Space.com that China’s national space agency contacted them for the first time about a possible orbital collision. “We are seeing the first steps,” Drew reportedly said.
Space debris is man-made junk in space, ranging from dead satellites and spent rocket stages to leftover fuel droplets and tiny parts or insulation materials that have broken off spacecraft. Even tools lost during spacewalks can become debris.


“In general, things are bad,” McDowell said, adding that the US, China and Russia were the major contributors to today’s debris environment.
As a pioneer in creating space debris, the US carried out the West Ford experiment in the 1960s, spreading more than 400 million tiny copper needles into orbit to form a reflective ring to safeguard long-distance communications during the Cold War.
The project failed to produce reliable signals, but it left behind one of the earliest and largest man-made debris clouds.
“There are still thousands of West Ford needles in orbit. They are in a belt between 2,000 and 5,000km (1,243 to 3,107 miles),” McDowell said.
China’s 2007 anti-satellite test is widely regarded as one of the largest debris-creating events in orbit. The strike shattered a defunct weather satellite into thousands of pieces, many of which remain in space today because of the high altitude of the break-up.
McDowell said he was slightly surprised the Tiangong hit happened at such a low altitude, where debris density is usually lower.
However, with internet megaconstellations rapidly packing low-Earth orbit – including SpaceX’s Starlink and China’s Guowang and Qianfan systems, each planned to exceed 10,000 satellites, “we can indeed expect more such incidents”, he added.

data.title: UK ex-PM Boris Johnson hits back at ‘incoherent’ Covid inquiry report
data.description:
Britain’s former prime minister Boris Johnson slammed an inquiry report critical of his government’s response to the Covid-19 pandemic as “hopelessly incoherent” in a social media post on Saturday.
“More than three years after the end of the pandemic, they are still wrangling about what went wrong,” Johnson, 61, also wrote in an article for the Daily Mail tabloid, days after the inquiry slammed his government for its “chaotic” response to the global pandemic costing thousands of lives.
The inquiry led by retired senior judge Heather Hallett said Johnson’s government “lacked” urgency in the early days of the health emergency, and that the first national lockdown came “too late”.
The UK, which went into lockdown later than most other European countries, suffered one of the worst Covid-19 death tolls on the continent, recording more than 128,500 fatalities by mid-July 2021.

UK families who lost their loved ones during the pandemic on Friday called for all privileges given to Johnson as a former prime minister to be removed.
Covid-19 Bereaved Families for Justice UK accused Johnson of “one of the gravest betrayals of the British public in modern history”.
“I repeat that I remain full of regret for the things the government I led got wrong and full of sympathy for all those who suffered – whether from the disease or from the steps we took to protect the population,” Johnson wrote in the article published late on Friday.
“All I can say is that everyone involved was doing our level best, under pretty difficult circumstances, to get it right and to save lives,” added Johnson, who commissioned the independent inquiry in 2021, when he was still leader.

However, he criticised the second report published by the inquiry on Thursday as “muddled” and “incoherent”, including for its position on lockdowns as being both devastating and necessary.
He also said it “failed” to answer two questions about the pandemic: “where did the virus come from – and were the lockdowns worth the terrible price we paid?”
He added that imposing restrictions before the government did at the end of March 2020, “would have been to contradict the scientific advice we were getting”.
“Namely that you had to make sure that you did not go too early because of the risk of exhausting public patience with the restrictions,” the former leader wrote.
